entry.title: Origins
entry.content:
「Origins」

  1. What is the universe? (Universe)

  2. What are humans, and what is human civilization? (Human)

  3. Who am I? (Ego)

Two questions I might have been exploring all along:

  1. What is the meaning of life?

  2. How can inner peace be achieved?

entry.title: Facts, Opinions, and Emotions
entry.content:
When we observe and understand the world, or when we describe and communicate, what exactly are we understanding and expressing?

Both facts and opinions need to be expressed, and emotions can naturally manifest in your choice of words and body language during expression.

Facts can be divided into “things” and “events.” A thing is an independent entity, while an event is the effect produced by a series of interactions between things.

An opinion is your description or perspective on a certain fact.

For example:

One morning, on your way to work, you saw a fox and took a picture with your phone. You were excited and immediately shared the experience with a friend.

You sent a message saying, “I saw a fox on my way to work today!”

Your friend might respond, “Really? How could there be a fox in the city?”

You then quickly sent the photo you took to confirm that what you said was true.

This led to a lively discussion between you and your friend about the incident.

Let’s break down this description process: What is a fact, what is an opinion, and what is an emotion?

The fox and the photo of the fox are facts.

Your friend’s question, “How could there be a fox in the city?” based on your description, is an opinion.

Excitement, doubt, and surprise (“I saw a fox!”) are emotions.

In fact, these are the main elements we use in our descriptions and expressions. But are facts reliable? Can facts that have been described still be considered reliable? People can easily fall into the emotions of subjective expression.

For instance, if you saw the fox but didn’t take a photo, what would you do when your friend asked you? Would your friend believe you without the photo?

Usually, if it’s a friend, they would trust you unconditionally and might even suggest that you take a photo next time. But if you were sharing this with a stranger, the result might not be the same.

This process actually involves “logic” and “critical thinking.” The example is relatively simple, but when we face more complex situations, we should be even clearer about what are the facts, what are the opinions, and what emotions are attached.

entry.title: Symbiosis
entry.content:
1. If the world only had humans left, would humanity eventually face extinction?

  2. If you were the last person on Earth, and you heard a knock on the door, would you choose to open it or not?

The second question was proposed by Einstein, but what I want to express here is, how would you feel if you woke up one day and found that the entire world no longer understands you, or if you couldn’t understand the world?

Last week, I finally started playing Pokémon Legends: Arceus, and it just so happens that the game touches on the worldview of Pokémon, where humans and Pokémon have a symbiotic relationship.

We humans are friends with Pokémon; we don’t need a hierarchical relationship.

From my understanding, whether it’s between humans, between humans and other species on Earth, or with some unknown civilization, it’s all about symbiosis.

After the outbreak of COVID-19, many people hoped that the pandemic would end quickly. Personally, I have always believed that COVID-19 will never completely go away, and what humanity should consider is how to coexist with the virus.

In this world, humans are not actually the rulers. In some ways, microorganisms or viruses are the true rulers of this world; humans are just hosts.

During team collaboration, I really appreciate the two principles mentioned by Zhang Yiming:

  1. Inside the company, no one should be addressed as “Chief.”

  2. Inside the company, you shouldn’t use the formal “您” to address others.

Often, what we seek is collective progress. Each person in a team or organization plays a role, and this role isn’t about who is more or less important. What matters is how everyone can use their abilities to achieve a common goal.

Understanding this helps you realize that whether it’s the boss, the founder, or the employees, they are all just roles, and each role has its own essential work.

For many years, I haven’t liked working in large companies, and I dislike being in a system even more. It’s not that these environments are bad, but I feel that they don’t suit me. I prefer an atmosphere where individual expression is maximized.

Recently, I’ve been thinking about another issue: “personal growth and organizational growth.”

I posed a hypothesis for myself: I believe that organizational growth accompanies personal growth, leading to a spiral development together.

So, how can personal and organizational growth happen simultaneously?

Sometimes it’s “collective wisdom,” and other times it’s “the madness of crowds.” What is the fundamental difference between the two?

In any case, I believe that symbiosis is the only way forward. Too few individuals can survive on their own. In the cold winter, everyone hopes to stay warm together, and the larger environment is no different.

This leads me to another question: Is there a self-contained, unified, and complete ultimate system? Because “entropy” always needs to be balanced by new energy. Where does this external energy come from?

Perhaps, before we approach the infinite boundary, all we can do is continuously assume that external energy is coming in endlessly.

So-called symbiosis, at present, seems to be about continuously acquiring external energy to seek internal balance and development.

entry.title: History·Ancestors
entry.content:
Why were those great ancestors, philosophers, and scientists so remarkable?

  1. Civilization progresses in stages, and each stage’s great figures solved problems by standing on the shoulders of the giants before them. This is one of the core reasons why humanity needs to continue developing and passing on knowledge.

For example: It was the philosophical and rational thinking of predecessors like Socrates and Aristotle that laid the foundation for logic, upon which all subsequent science is based.

  1. I believe that every generation of humanity grapples with a few fundamental questions:

a). Who am I? An exploration of self-awareness.

b). Who am I with you? An exploration of social relationships.

c). What is the universe? From astrophysics to microscopic particles, an exploration of the world. All in one.

I believe that ways of thinking can be cultivated and have a top-level structure, aligning with first principles. Cognition requires accumulation, and with each era—be it the ancient times, the Stone Age, the Mechanical Age, the Electrical Age, or the Information Age—the problems discovered or solved have been continuously advanced based on the technological productivity of the time.

When we look at individuals within the grand flow of human development, the emergence of great figures is inevitable; they are products of their environment. As for why they were great and we are not (yet), I still believe it comes down to the technological advantages and cognitive awakening brought about by their era’s unique circumstances. I recently revisited Einstein’s “The World As I See It” and have been pondering what they were doing during their time, and why they thought the way they did.

I am particularly fond of Einstein’s statement that the world is comprehensible.

There are still many unresolved questions and many scientists continuously researching and exploring the fundamental nature of this world.

entry.title: Dimensions | Point·Line·Plane | Motion
entry.content:
To an ant, the world might seem flat. Humans also once believed the Earth was flat.

A line is formed by connecting a series of points, and a plane is made up of multiple lines.

When we talk about “dimension elevation” or “dimension reduction,” what exactly is this dimension?

Why does the view from a mountaintop make all other mountains seem small? Why do humans easily mock the pitiful nature of insects? And why do we sometimes look back at our past selves and find our previous ignorance laughable?

We often place great importance on a single exam, the success or failure of a startup, or the joys and sorrows of life. Are these things important? Yes, they are, because they form the lines of your life experience, shaping the three-dimensional you.

“Material is in motion.”

This is a fundamental law. We often say that we should learn to view problems dynamically. It’s easy to see that when you move a point from A to B, it forms a line. When you move a line from A to B, it forms a plane. When that plane moves from A to B, it becomes a solid.

“Material motion requires force, and moving against resistance consumes energy.”

One day, a person who felt aimless suddenly realized they needed a dream. This “realization,” whether driven by external or internal factors, is a kind of “force,” propelling them toward that dream.

What is a dream? A dream is a leap in one’s life, a determination and journey from a lower dimension to a higher one.

What is resistance? It is the countless energy expenditures required to reach a higher dimension, representing the various experiences of life.

Life should be three-dimensional. It requires curiosity, continuous exploration, and constant experiences.

Life is a journey of cultivation, a process of pursuing the other shore.

When you understand that many times you need to observe problems dynamically, from a comprehensive, three-dimensional, and even higher-dimensional perspective, you may find that the answers to many things become clear.

entry.title: Suddenly Looking Back, That Person Is at the Lantern Festival
entry.content:
“State·Landscape”

Recently, discussions about “higher dimensions” often refer to an elevation in one’s state of mind.

One of my favorite Zen sayings is:

“Seeing a mountain is just seeing a mountain, seeing water is just seeing water;

Seeing a mountain is no longer just a mountain, seeing water is no longer just water;

Seeing a mountain is once again seeing a mountain, seeing water is once again seeing water.”

At various turning points in life, I like to reflect on this saying. When I first read it during my school years, I understood it, but gradually realized that I only grasped it on a superficial level. After living for half my life and experiencing many things, I found that much of the wisdom behind it comes from “experience.”

When new things appear, our first reaction is often to see only the surface—this is a mountain, this is water. Curiosity might drive us to explore, or these things might simply intrude into our lives.

When you delve deeper into the characteristics of these things, you might discover they differ from your initial perception.

A good example from life: We have learned so many Chinese characters since elementary school. While recognizing characters in paragraphs and sentences is straightforward, seeing a single character in isolation might suddenly feel unfamiliar, even leading to doubts about whether it’s written correctly.

Many aspects of life and work are similar. What seems like a simple job might become more complex when actually undertaken. Can you do it well? Can you maintain it year after year, day after day? When time is added into the equation, things might become less straightforward, and you may find yourself re-evaluating them.

When you start to seriously address a matter; when you analyze it thoroughly from beginning to end, from inside to out; and even when you take it on as a lifelong pursuit, you will become increasingly at ease.

Mr. Wang Guowei also summarized the three realms of life:

  1. Last night’s west wind withered the green tree; climbing the high tower alone, gazing at the road to the horizon.
  2. The belt grows wider, yet no regret; I have withered away for her.
  3. Searching for him a thousand times in the crowd, suddenly looking back, that person is at the lantern festival.

In Chinese discussions of states, people often say that they are only to be felt and not explained. This may be because the experience is personal, and only one’s own experience can reveal its true essence.

The Zen Master Huineng said:

“Without a Bodhi tree, and the bright mirror is not a platform;

Originally, there is nothing, where can dust cling?”

Perhaps after you have traveled through landscapes, looking back, you will find that it is all but a gentle cloud, which is the essence of completeness.

entry.title: The Essence of Education
entry.content:
A few days ago, I came across a short video discussing “What is the essence of education?” I found it quite insightful, so I’d like to share it:

———

When Michelangelo completed the statue of David, he was astounded, and so was everyone else.

A journalist asked Michelangelo, “How did you create such a magnificent work?”

He didn’t answer immediately. Instead, he pondered for a long time before carefully responding:

“It wasn’t I who created this great work; David was already inside the stone. I simply set him free.”

These words are brilliant, capturing the timeless essence of education.

“Every person has a better self, perhaps even a great self, within them. Every child undoubtedly possesses such a self.”

———

When it comes to education, I believe it’s important to consider two roles: the educator and the learner.

For educators—whether they are teachers or elders—I appreciate the approach of encouragement and discovery. Just like in the video, the idea that “David” was already within the stone suggests that educators should first believe that they are merely guides, playing a role in passing on knowledge. The future will belong to the younger generation, as this is ingrained in our species’ genetics.

Many educators prefer to impose authority and suppress with their status, which is not ideal. Young people have their own consciousness and think about issues from different perspectives. We should communicate as equals, discussing the logic of the matter itself.

Educators are educators primarily because they have taken steps ahead, possessing more experience, or perhaps they have stronger or more specialized skills. However, these “experiences” or “skills” are not necessarily correct and should be open to discussion. It is through this process that educators hope learners will ultimately acquire these experiences to create more value.

For learners, the most crucial element is “confidence”—to believe that they are unique and excellent. Of course, confidence should not be blind or arrogant; it should be built on continuous self-awareness and learning, grounded in a scientific and dialectical attitude.

I believe two insights are particularly important:

  1. Success is built on the improvement gained through multiple failures and requires deliberate practice.

The more valuable something is, the more effort and resistance it usually involves. I’ve always believed that things easily obtained in this world are rare; occasional success might come from sheer luck, but one cannot rely entirely on such rare events. Instead, it’s essential to continually experience and try, to enhance one’s comprehensive abilities across multiple fields, and to strengthen the capacity for survival and growth. This ability requires constant learning and adaptation—lifelong learning for lifelong growth.

  1. Positive psychological feedback from oneself and the environment, such as praise and encouragement.

Often, success is not guaranteed but possible. However, the subconscious influence of positive psychology can sometimes help one persevere, and persistence often leads to success. Nevertheless, it’s also important to consider “sunk costs.” If too much time and effort have been invested in something without success, it might be worth reconsidering further investment—a common mistake in entrepreneurship.

Both educators and learners should first gain a clearer understanding of themselves and then of others and the organizations they are part of. We must discover our true selves and learn to unearth the “true self” in others, so that human civilization can continue to be passed down.

entry.title: Uncertainty
entry.content:
People everywhere seek stability, yet the world is often unpredictable.

The COVID-19 outbreak in Shanghai seemed sudden, and the lockdown appeared to come out of nowhere. Every day, new cases emerge, and the lockdown, originally scheduled to end on the 5th, is still in question on the 8th. Many people have run out of stockpiled food, or never had any to begin with. The delivery services, such as couriers and takeout, are overwhelmed, and neighborhood committees are struggling to keep up with organizing and managing the community. It feels like chaos in a city as large as Shanghai.

In an uncertain environment, the most crucial thing is the ability to manage risk—the ability to be antifragile.

“Every entity benefits from or is harmed by volatility. Fragility is the condition of being harmed by uncertainty and volatility, while antifragility is the ability to avoid these harms and even benefit from chaos and uncertainty.”

First and foremost, I believe that an important aspect of antifragility is “mindset.” A positive and optimistic attitude is vital. As mentioned in the book Antifragile, there’s a famous quote by Nietzsche: “That which does not kill me makes me stronger.”

In this battle against the pandemic, while many people are complaining and criticizing, those with responsibility have already stepped up to analyze and solve problems on the front lines.

In most cases, the fundamental reason for failure in the face of difficulties is the collapse of one’s will and mindset.

Secondly, I believe it’s essential to have a “crisis awareness.”

Take stockpiling food, for instance. Some families stock up a considerable amount—not through panic buying, but in a controlled manner. If the lockdown was expected to end on the 5th but didn’t, what would you do? At the very least, you would stockpile more essentials like rice, oil, salt, and eggs just in case.

I’ve always believed in one principle: prepare for the worst in any situation.

At work, if your team tells you a project will be completed by the 10th, you should mentally prepare for the possibility that it might not be. Do you have backup time? Should you push the progress ahead?

Over the past few years of the COVID-19 pandemic, many companies across various industries have struggled to survive, with many eventually closing down. But New Oriental managed to stay afloat because Yu Minhong prepared for this from day one.

Playing Texas Hold’em is a good exercise for this mindset; it’s all about probabilities. Occasionally taking risks is fine, but if you constantly go all-in, the result is inevitably a loss.

Uncertainty is, in fact, the norm. The factors influencing outcomes are multidimensional and probabilistic; achieving 100% certainty is nearly impossible. What we need to learn is how to thrive and grow in the face of uncertainty and unpredictability. This is a fundamental skill.

Consider reading Antifragile and delving deeper into this ability.

entry.title: Life Thrives on Negative Entropy Embrace the Pain
entry.content:
Pain is the sensation of suffering or a profound emotional impact.

Growth is a process of continually stepping out of your comfort zone. Staying in the comfort zone for too long can lead to a loss of critical thinking, an avoidance of reality, and a lack of crisis awareness.

Schrödinger once said, “Living is fighting against the increase of entropy; life thrives on negative entropy.”

This is one of my favorite quotes—both pessimistic and romantic.

Embracing pain is a form of reverence for life. Life itself is about “living towards death,” and life inherently embodies “impermanence.” Only by truly understanding these concepts can we unleash a powerful force for living.

Watching movies, listening to music, reading, exercising—there are many ways to keep both the mind and body in a state of “heartbeat.”

Keep the pain, keep the insights, live towards the light, and the future will only become more brilliant.

entry.title: I Am What I Am, a Firework of a Different Color
entry.content:
Formal Logic states that a concept is a form of thought that reflects the “unique” attributes (inherent or essential properties) of an object.

Recently, I’ve been exploring a question: Is there a way for personal breakthrough?

Continuous learning, organizing notes, building knowledge systems, cognitive awakening, and so on. Yet, I still feel unsettled, as if daily problems remain unsolved, always in doubt, always exploring.

In work communication between superiors and subordinates, it often seems like you’ve explained something clearly, but the final result differs significantly. When we reflect on it, we often find that there were discrepancies in how we understood certain concepts or things.

One day, while reading, I came across Einstein’s statement: “The most incomprehensible thing about the world is that it is comprehensible.” Having been puzzled by some issues for a long time, I was deeply moved by the word “comprehensible.”

Later, I realized that one way to understand the world is through “logic,” through induction and deduction, and the foundation of logic is “concepts.”

Thus, the paradigm for understanding the world, in my view, becomes quite simple: Have you truly understood a “concept”?

The most crucial aspect of a concept is its “characteristic,” the key factor that distinguishes it from other things. This understanding makes many problems much clearer.

The internet often mentions that if you can’t explain your product in one sentence, it’s not a qualified product. In business, this is about “positioning,” while in logic, it’s about “concept” description, a process of “definition.”

Many people say they can’t express themselves well. The reason for this is often that they haven’t thought things through; it’s a mess. The reason for this mess is unclear logic, and unclear logic is likely due to unclear “concepts.”

I now enjoy a new way of note-taking with Flomo: just recording “keywords.” This method is quick and efficient. On one hand, it provides a clue and facilitates indexing; on the other hand, it helps practice two logical abilities: “deduction” and “abstraction.” Later, when I have time, I can elaborate on these concepts, possibly leading to a small blog post.

Deduction involves expanding on a concept, making it more understandable, defining it, or providing examples. Abstraction involves simplifying complex matters, extracting commonalities, and being as concise as possible, which is the foundation of structured expression.

At last year’s NoteMan Annual Conference, Teacher Ke Zhou specifically mentioned “conceptual thinking,” a way of thinking. I looked it up and found that Huawei’s Teacher Su Guanzhe also frequently discusses it.

Conceptual Thinking is the ability to identify the essential characteristics of seemingly unrelated things.

In this sense, my understanding is that it is a thinking ability that sees the essence through phenomena. But it seems I haven’t explained it very clearly because there are many ways to see the essence through phenomena. This isn’t the unique trait of “conceptual thinking.” I’ve asked Teacher Ke Zhou some questions about this, and I’ll update when I receive new answers.

Personally, I believe that trying to understand the world is about understanding the concepts of all things. Understanding concepts is understanding the world.

Leslie Cheung sings, “I am what I am, a firework of a different color.”

If “I” want to live a brilliant and independent life, I need to have my own characteristics, whether it’s individuality or value output. Having that unique aspect helps you understand “self.”

entry.title: Supporting Role or Leading Role?
entry.content:
Life is essentially a journey of playing different roles.

Many people’s first experience of transformation is the sudden realization that, though they thought they were the main character in their story, they were actually just a supporting role, or even merely a passerby.

As a child, I used to enjoy watching the main characters in movies. But as I grew older, I found myself more interested in the supporting roles, learning about the growth experiences of the smaller characters.

If life were a play, and the director only gave you 10 seconds, how would you seize that moment?

We play many roles throughout our lives, but often, we don’t fully understand these roles. Some roles seem to be handed to us unexpectedly.

We are born as sons or daughters, we go to school and become students, then we work and become employees, eventually rising to executive roles. We fall in love, get married, and become lovers, then eventually grandparents or take on many other roles.

But do we truly understand the meaning and responsibility behind each role?

One of my deepest reflections on roles came when I was first promoted to director. I was excited about the promotion and the raise, but when I started working in that role, managing both up and down the chain was incredibly challenging. For the first few months, I struggled, trying to handle everything personally, unable to prioritize effectively. My boss criticized me, my subordinates pushed back, and my team was in disarray.

Then one day, I suddenly understood what being a “director” really meant. I grasped the nature of the role, set clear boundaries, and focused on the essential tasks. Slowly, things started to improve. If you can’t quickly adapt to a new role, both you and your colleagues will feel exhausted.

What does it mean to be an employee? What does it mean to be a manager?

A true manager is someone who is willing to think proactively, take responsibility, guide others, and take action, rather than just giving orders. How many of your skills as a manager could actually surpass those of the specialists on the front line?

In life, what does it mean to be a child or a parent? A student or a teacher? A friend or a lover? Where are the boundaries of these roles, and what do we expect from the other person in each relationship?

Too often, we confuse our roles or lack clarity in them, or we passively fall into a role without taking it on actively.

Recently, there was a viral short video about a war game played abroad. The red and blue teams were battling for territory, and one side was retreating steadily. In the end, their commander logged off and fled. At that moment, a Chinese player stepped up and took over the command, leading the team to victory with bilingual English-Chinese instructions.

This Chinese player may have started the game simply wanting to play as a soldier, but when the moment came, they took on the role of commander and performed it perfectly.

The key here is “attentiveness” in daily life—paying attention, thinking carefully, daring to try and take responsibility. Only then can you rise to the occasion when it counts.

Many roles in life are assigned to us. If that’s the case, then let’s play those roles well. If one day the director gives you a 10-second role, will you seize it?

Wong Jing once remarked that Stephen Chow showed seven different expressions in a 10-second scene in A Chinese Odyssey. How much effort do you think went into perfecting those seven expressions?

entry.title: I Think, Therefore I Am
entry.content:
Descartes once said, “I think, therefore I am.”

Many years ago, I asked a question on Zhihu about how to understand Hegel’s phrase “What is real is rational.” For years, it seems I’ve been chasing the question: If my existence is rational, then why do I exist, and what makes my existence different?

Life, at times, seems to be a series of hypotheses—some societal, some personal. What follows is an endless pursuit and exploration surrounding these assumptions.

Lately, I’ve been reading two books: The Essence and The Power of Thinking. My interest in the concept of “essence” sparked this journey. Most of us seek to understand the essence of things, but it’s often elusive. So why can some people perceive the essence, while others cannot?

Most people recognize the importance of thinking—independent thinking, critical thinking, deep thinking, and reverse thinking. Yet, despite various thought processes being distilled over time, humanity has never fully explained the act of thinking itself, such as what consciousness is.

The Power of Thinking offers this definition of thinking: Thinking refers to the cognitive process of analyzing and judging one or more external objects to arrive at specific conclusions about them.

One question I’ve pondered for a long time is, “How can we understand the world?”

It’s a process that reflects the world onto the individual, moving from the objective world to the subjective, and from this shore to the other shore. It’s challenging but worth a lifetime of thought.

What you see in the world may not be true. It requires continuous verification to determine what is real. Some find this pursuit dull, but I find it deeply meaningful.

Biologically, thinking is essentially the result of nerve impulses between neurons, leading to reflexes and other behaviors.

We think every day, passively as information floods in, actively when faced with new problems, and sometimes unconsciously, even when our minds seem to wander.

But how should we think? How can we make our thinking more efficient? How can we turn our thoughts into actions?

While thinking may have no end, it can have dimensions—depth, breadth, boundaries, and stages. Thinking can even become reflexive, where we think about thinking itself—meta-thinking.

Sometimes understanding the rules of the world is simple, just a short journey from the object itself to the brain’s specific response. Yet this brief path is fraught with challenges, and one misstep could lead you astray, away from your intended destination.

In my view, human civilization persists because it is the crystallization of continuous thought and exploration by those who came before us. Sometimes I wonder, when you fully understand the thought processes of past greats, is that person who thinks just like them still you?

Schopenhauer said, “The man who thinks stands as the sovereign of his own spiritual kingdom.” May every thoughtful person reign over their own world and become the ruler of their own life.

[1] Ai Fei. The Essence Link

[2] Lüska. The Power of Thinking Link

entry.title: Habit is a Behavior, and Behavior is Easier to Change
entry.content:
A few days ago, I came across a short psychology video on Douyin from “This Thought Official” titled “What Should You Never Say to Your Child?” It was quite insightful.

Guest Huang Shiming answered: Negative language at the identity level should not be used.

For example, if a client says to me, “Hey, teacher, I’m an alcoholic,” and seeks psychological counseling.

I won’t agree with that. I would say, “Hey, are you saying that you have a habit of drinking?”

Habit is a behavior, and behavior is easier to change.

If someone says, “I’m a depressed person.”

I would respond, “Are you saying you smile a little less than others?”

If my child says, “Dad, I’m sure I’ll fail. I identify as someone who can’t succeed.”

I would say, “Oh, are you saying that this time, you might fail?”

This relaxes the tension (deep breath) and opens up a space for relaxation, where new things can come in.

Of course, they might still fail the exam tomorrow, but now we share an alliance, a common intention, to see how things can get a little better than they are now. I become that ally in my child’s eyes, the person they believe in.

———

Many times, whether labeling ourselves or others, it’s easy to apply definitive labels, such as “I’m a loser,” or “He’s a bad person.”

Carrying such a definitive label can be an invisible burden for years, sometimes suffocating. If you’re lucky, you might overcome it, but if not, it can become a vicious cycle.

Life has no boundaries, there aren’t as many labels as we think, and things shouldn’t be so easily defined. The world isn’t just black and white, good or bad. Often, time is the best companion, and a positive mindset can change a lot.

Video Source

entry.title: The Process of Thinking Involves the Use of Concepts, Judgments, and Reasoning
entry.content:
We often talk about logic, but what exactly is logic?

The word “logic” is derived from the Greek word λσγοS (logos), originally meaning thought, reason, principle, word, etc. In everyday language, “logic” can have three different meanings:

First, it refers to the regularity of the interconnection and development of objective things, as well as the regularity of human thinking that reflects this regularity. For example, “the logic of the Chinese revolution” or “the logic of things.” Here, “logic” refers to the regularity of objective things. Similarly, “to draw a logical conclusion” or “the article has strong logic” refers to the regularity of thinking.

Second, it represents a particular theory, viewpoint, or method of looking at things. For example, invaders often label their aggression as “friendship,” which we denounce as “pure robber logic.” In this context, “logic” refers to the invaders’ twisted theory, viewpoint, or method of reasoning.

Third, it refers to the science that studies the forms and laws of correct thinking, i.e., formal logic and dialectical logic, though it is commonly used to refer to formal logic. For instance, “learn some grammar and logic” or “high school students should study logic.” Here, “logic” refers to the science of formal logic.

So, what is “formal logic”?

Formal logic studies the forms and laws of thinking and is, first and foremost, a science that studies thinking. Engels said that logic is the doctrine of the laws of the thinking process itself.

And what is “thinking,” and how does human thinking take place?

Mao Zedong explained in his “On Practice”: People initially see only the phenomena of various things in the process, the one-sidedness of each thing, and the external connections between things… This belongs to the perceptual stage of human cognition. At this stage, “people cannot yet form profound concepts or draw logical conclusions.” However, as social practice continues, the things that arouse sensations and impressions in people are repeatedly experienced, leading to a sudden transformation (a leap) in the cognitive process, resulting in the formation of concepts.

Recently, I was also unclear about the origin of “concepts,” and this passage explains it well.

The process of thinking is an abstract, indirect reflection of the objective world, characterized by abstraction and indirectness. This reflection is realized through language.

The thinking process involves the use of concepts, judgments, and reasoning, making these the essential forms of thinking used to reflect objective reality, which logic refers to as the forms of thought.

———

Most of the content above is derived from the book Formal Logic, compiled by the Department of Philosophy at East China Normal University. The book has reached its fifth edition. I’ve read the first edition, which was initially drafted in 1963. The fifth edition has improved significantly compared to the first.

Why am I interested in this book? The main reason is that I have always been contemplating how to correctly understand the world.

Formal logic focuses on the formal laws of thinking, without concern for the content itself. This aligns with what I’ve been pondering—is there a fixed paradigm for the reflection of the objective world to the subjective world? To me, logic is the underlying layer, consisting of three elements: concepts, judgments, and reasoning.

Science may not always be right, but it is, at least for now, the worldview I am committed to.

Sometimes I think that the education system in China is really lacking in the widespread teaching of logic. After becoming certain of this path, I might even become a preacher of logic someday.

In a sense, correctly understanding the world means understanding the dialectical relationship between me and the world—a spiraling, reciprocating path from the this-worldly to the otherworldly, a path of mutual interaction between consciousness and matter.

entry.title: What You Focus on Echoes Back
entry.content:
Rebirth

There’s a classic paradox in philosophy known as Theseus’ Ship. If the wooden planks of Theseus’ ship are gradually replaced until none of the original planks remain, is it still the same ship?

Some say yes, others say no. If our cells regenerate every day, and eventually, all of our cells are replaced, are we still the same person? And if our thoughts and perceptions evolve every day, are we still the same self?

Perhaps the answer doesn’t really matter. What matters is that life goes on, and new beginnings arise.

Material conditions shape consciousness, but consciousness also impacts material reality. A new understanding might lead to a form of rebirth.

In English, the prefix “re” signifies repetition or renewal, as in words like reborn, rework, rebuild, review, restart, and recall.

In the digital age, when new technological productivity emerges, every industry can be reinvented. From web1.0 to web2.0, and now the buzz around web3.0, each phase has led to the rebirth of various sectors.

For individuals, many of our perceptions are influenced by our upbringing and environment. However, these perceptions aren’t necessarily correct. Human behavior is driven by habits, and habits are shaped by consciousness. So, should we not re-examine our past knowledge, concepts, and beliefs?

This might be a long journey, but certain key ideas are worth re-evaluating. Over the past few years, I’ve been searching for the root of certain issues, and recently found answers in two books. Interestingly, both are textbooks: one is Formal Logic, and the other is Marxist Philosophy. Perhaps my curiosity and exposure to these subjects in college led me to this path of introspection.

In simple terms, it’s a re-evaluation of the Three Views: worldview, values, and outlook on life.

Marxist philosophy teaches that the relationship between humans and the world revolves around two main activities: understanding the world and transforming it. All human activities are centered on these two goals. This also explains why, in retrospect, “carving oneself and benefiting others” represents a fundamental drive and value. First, we must understand ourselves and the world, and then we transform it.

Everyone has the opportunity for a rebirth, but this opportunity requires a re-examination of oneself and the world—a baptism of the Three Views.

In my value system, only through a deep understanding of oneself and the world can we find a peaceful path forward.

Explore more on Rebirth, Philosophy, Worldview, Values, and Outlook on Life.

entry.title: Many Times, What We Say is Just a Cliché
entry.content:
Seek Truth from Facts

If a phrase is repeated over and over until no one knows what it means anymore, it may become just a cliché.

Back in high school, we had a debate. During the argument, both sides kept mentioning worldview. Finally, one of our opponents asked, “We keep talking about ‘worldview’, but can your side explain what exactly is a worldview?” We were stunned and couldn’t answer. The entire class was present, and even our teacher, who had been quietly listening, applauded when this question was asked.

This debate left a deep impression on me, especially regarding the question: What is a worldview? Another realization was that many concepts we think we understand well and can easily talk about are often just clichés, repeated without deep thought.

What Does “Seek Truth from Facts” Mean?

The Book of Han records: “Study diligently, love antiquity, and seek truth from facts.”

Mao Zedong also explained “seeking truth from facts”: “Facts” refer to all objectively existing things. “Truth” refers to the internal connections of these objective things, that is, their regularity. “Seek” means we study them.”

Often, when we approach and solve problems, we rely on subjective perceptions, only on experience, without investigation, and sometimes based on unfounded sources.

What are facts, what are opinions, what is objective, and what is subjective? Only by basing our analysis on these objective facts can we rationally analyze and solve problems. The foundation of critical thinking is based on facts. We often talk about the madness of crowds and collective wisdom, but what matters is whether the conclusions drawn are based on facts or on opinions.

What kind of world do you understand? What kind of world do you believe in? Try to clarify these slowly, and many problems will naturally be resolved.

entry.title: The Dao in the Balance of Yin and Yang
entry.content:
“Yin and Yang”

As the ancient saying goes: “The Dao is the balance of Yin and Yang.”

Sometimes, the pursuit of perfection and persistent reflection can not only validate our existence but also bring profound self-awareness.

In constructing my personal knowledge system using Flomo, I have gone through several stages:

  1. Classic Notes: Minimal reflection, random keyword tags, no classification.
  2. Increased Reflection: Less note-taking, categorized tags [P.A.R.A], structured expression.
  3. Contrasting Thinking: Considering facts vs. opinions, rational vs. emotional, objective vs. subjective, explicit vs. implicit, self vs. others, individual vs. society, ordered vs. disordered, positive vs. negative, etc.
  4. Form and Content: Reflecting on form vs. content, logical thinking, tracing philosophy, and conceptual thinking.
  5. Exploration and Simplification: Contemplating the here and now vs. the beyond, exploring the principle of simplicity, pursuing a picturesque realm.
  6. Abstract and Deductive: Thinking in abstract vs. deductive terms.
  7. …

Currently, my reflections have not yet reached the ideal pinnacle of Dao, nor have I touched the boundary of that metaphorical door. Each time I read the profound insights of ancient sages from hundreds or even thousands of years ago, I feel immense joy and even find myself moved to tears, experiencing a deep sense of luck.

In Season 2 of KQ Walk the World, there’s a hypothesis: What would be the consequences if deserts disappeared from Earth?

While turning deserts into forests might seem like a grand achievement, the presence of deserts contributes to the global climate cycle. If deserts were to turn into forests, it could potentially create new ecological disasters for the Earth.

Are justice and evil absolute? Is right and wrong truly important? Is there a gray area between black and white? Are rational numbers finite? Do irrational numbers have an end? Why is energy conserved? Why does entropy continue to increase?

The world of computers consists of only 0 and 1, while the real world is a balance of Yin and Yang, constantly rotating and alternating.

Perhaps the boundary between reality and virtuality is merely a matter of perception. What defines the point where quantity transforms into quality?

entry.title: Useless and Uselessness
entry.content:
“Dao”

This weekend, I revisited the TV series The Dao and came across a profound discussion about the concept of “Dao”:

———

Ding Yuanying said: “There are three levels to understanding society: technology, systems, and culture. Whether for an individual or a nation, any fate ultimately stems from cultural attributes. Dominant cultures create strong individuals, while weak cultures produce weak ones. This is a rule, and it can be understood as **the Dao, which is not subject to human will.**”

“Dominant cultures follow the laws of things, while weak cultures rely on the moral expectations of the strong, seeking salvation. Dominant cultures are like ‘secret manuals’ in martial arts, while weak cultures, being easier to learn, understand, and use, become popular.”

Rui Xiaodan asked: “I can understand this from the literal sense, but how does it apply?”

Ding Yuanying replied: “**Useless and Uselessness.**”

———

The phrase “Useless and Uselessness” precisely addresses the answer to my recent intellectual dilemma.

The term “Dao” represents the principles I have long sought, things that are not subject to personal will, which could be laws, ways of thinking, or various forms.

Through prolonged exploration, I have come to believe in the foundational role of Formal Logic and the worldview of Marxist Philosophy.

Even though I have established logic and Marxist philosophy as foundational driving forces, I still found myself confused in practical application. Perhaps this is because the process of applying these theories in practice often brings new insights.

I have questioned how logic and philosophy can be applied in real life. Rui Xiaodan’s question, “What’s the use?” highlights this concern.

Useless might seem to have no direct application, but Uselessness aligns with a belief I have long held, although I had not fully committed to it before.

The boundary of Uselessness lies in the fact that logic and philosophy can indeed be applied to various aspects of daily life and work.

I feel fortunate to have gained some insight in my 30s. This insight has provided a clear answer from the “Dao”. The answer has been within me all along, only needing a reason to believe and persist.

An interesting question is: Humans often seek meaning in their actions, but is this meaning truly important?

I’m still not sure, but for many things, it’s crucial for me to understand “why”.

The concept of “Useless and Uselessness” embodies the principle of simplicity. What seems simple is actually challenging to achieve, while complex things can be simplified, often due to our misunderstanding or overcomplication of the unknown.

Laozi said: “Wu wei but nothing is not done.”

Following the “Dao”, seeking truth from facts, and observing the cause and effect in our actions.

entry.title: Why Do Humans Ask "Why"?
entry.content:
Seeing mountains as mountains, and water as water;

Seeing mountains as not mountains, and water as not water;

Seeing mountains still as mountains, and water still as water.

At first, humans might not have asked “why.” But when did humans consciously start asking “why”? This is the origin.

Why can the 「2W1H」 framework help us think through certain problems, and why does this framework clarify some matters?

What is What, what is Why, and what is How?

Why are there three, and why are these three?

We are thinking, but we are rarely aware that we are thinking.

Where is the source of thought? In the brain.

Where is the source of the brain? In consciousness.

One of philosophy’s focuses might be on the origin.

If humans ask why, what might result? They might obtain an effect.

If humans don’t ask why, what might happen? They might just follow a predetermined path.

We continuously strive to explore the origin of this world, constantly asking why in order to understand the world clearly. We are all exploring the rationality of existence. There is always something implicit behind this: 「causal logic.」

It’s difficult for us to explain how the brain expressed and understood the world before consciousness, how children understand the world, how they think, or if they even think at all. Is our thinking as adults a kind of imprisoned thinking?

This world seems to have a “cause” for everything. The moment humans ask a question, there might be an unalterable belief within consciousness: belief in “causality.”

I still vividly remember a scene from “Divine Providence” where Han Xin tries to understand and believe in the existence of aliens. Later, in “New Song,” I was amazed by Wang Anshi’s awareness that even if someone knew the future, it wouldn’t be enough to change anything.

A possible law of this world is that 「everything has a cause and effect.」

But, is that true?

Does an absolute belief in causality prevent you from truly understanding the root of the world? Just as what we call absolute might just be a special case of a probabilistic event, this world might be primarily composed of dark matter, with matter being only a part of it.

When what we firmly believe to be the essence is merely a facade, will you still believe in this world?

entry.title: Assuming the World Is Comprehensible, Where Lies Its Comprehensibility?
entry.content:
Comprehension

The most incomprehensible thing about the world is that it is comprehensible.
— Albert Einstein

What exactly was Albert Einstein’s understanding of 「comprehension」 regarding this world?

Was it quantum mechanics, probabilistic randomness, or something else? Einstein might not have originally said this quote; it could have been beautified by the media. But where exactly does this 「domain」 of “comprehension” lie?

He said, “God does not play dice.”

But the behavior of microscopic particles is determined by probability. Randomness and probability are the norms of this world.

The macroscopic world is no different. Behind countless decisions and actions lie various complex events and conditions influencing the outcome.

Socrates

All men are mortal;

Socrates is a man;

Therefore, Socrates is mortal.

What if probability was suddenly added behind inductive and deductive logic?

99% of all men are mortal;

Socrates has an 80% chance of being a man;

So, is Socrates mortal? What is the likelihood of his mortality?

Randomness Is Not the Same as Uncertainty

The biggest difference between randomness and uncertainty lies in whether the possible outcomes of an event are known.

Randomness means that I know all the possible outcomes of an event, but I don’t know which outcome will occur next.

I once thought this was process thinking, meaning that when you’ve experienced something, you can clearly see the entire process of its development. By looking through the river of history or even understanding it from a God’s-eye view, many things seem natural.

But unfortunately, it doesn’t seem to be that way. For instance, why do different ideologies and camps exist? Even our Earth might only be a random existence.

Our expectations for the future and our attempts to understand future uncertainties hinge on continuously transforming uncertainties into randomness, because most things in this world are random, not uncertain.

God might enjoy playing dice, but we’re fortunate if we know that the dice only have numbers 1 through 6. However, if we don’t know whether the numbers on God’s dice might exceed 6, that’s uncertainty, and it even sounds a bit terrifying.

The Endpoint of Probability Is 1

Probability has three properties:

First, probability is always between 0 and 1; it can never be negative.

Second, the sum of the probabilities of all basic events in the sample space is 1.

Third, the probability of a random event not occurring is equal to 1 minus the probability of it occurring.

The second property is particularly interesting: the sum of probabilities is 1, or 100%. It seems obvious, but this “1” has two meanings.

First, our sample must be complete, without omissions, which is difficult to achieve in reality.

Second, probability is a necessary event under a system, but a contingent event under details.

To make this event as likely as possible to occur, one must delve deeply into understanding it.

entry.title: Jet Li's Quest: Yin Yang, Science & Pure Dreams
entry.content:
Lately, intermittently, I’ve come across a classic interview by Wang Lifen about Jet Li. When I watched the complete video again, I found myself reflecting on a multitude of topics—thinking, the One Foundation, the Internet, humanity, faith, and so much more. This led to a new understanding and sentiment.

Jet Li’s Early Understanding of Yin Yang

Jet Li understood the concept of Yin Yang when he was only 11 years old, grasping the essence of Tai Chi. It wasn’t until he was around 30 that he began to glimpse the truth: the world isn’t just black and white.

The Relationship Between Science and Truth

Speaking of science, Jet Li shared:

"The mysteries of the universe that humanity has mastered over five thousand years are truly limited. The scientific knowledge we possess is quite confined; it's called science precisely because it isn't scientific."

I never expected to hear this from the Emperor of Kung Fu, but it’s truly remarkable that someone who didn’t graduate from elementary school, who has remained humble about his lack of education, could arrive at such a profound realization.

Regarding science, many people nowadays lack an understanding, thinking that science equates to truth. In fact, it’s not. The correctness of science is temporary, and it’s through constant negation and revision that we arrive at truth. There might not even be any absolute truth in this world. The only truth might be the continuous process of seeking the truth.

Exploring Zen Buddhism and the Path of Dao

Due to martial arts and the film “Shaolin Temple,” Jet Li gained insight into the world, comprehending movies and the universe. As a teenager, he developed an entirely new perception of the world, which he continually pondered. He questioned why people always said, “Martial artists are simple-minded with well-developed limbs.” He spent half his life seeking evidence and disproving this notion, dedicating himself to learning to cultivate as much wisdom as possible, which he indeed achieved.

This realization motivated Jet Li to leave the system, even if it meant breaking his leg. His determination led to a serendipitous leg injury, and he found himself ecstatic about it, sensing that fate was on his side. Perhaps it was youthful ignorance or youthful aspiration that led him to such an outlook. He ventured out, giving birth to numerous exceptional films, but films were merely a fraction of his excellence.

He is someone who contemplates deeply: he reflects on whether something is problematic when everyone does it. He ponders how to be more efficient. These two ways of thinking became deeply ingrained in him at a young age, perhaps influenced by his early encounter with the concept of yin and yang. It’s remarkable; although exposed to this concept early, not everyone can grasp its essence.

Fortunately, after his introduction to Zen Buddhism, the constant yearning led him to the edge of Dao, a stroke of immense luck.

The Pursuit of Pure Dreams

He continuously reads books, biographies of famous people, the life stories of Marie Curie, Stalin, Hitler, Edison, and selections from Deng Xiaoping, among others. He reads classics, including the Bible and the Quran.

When he was young, Jet Li had a dream—to water flowers. However, when he shared this dream with those around him, they labeled him as lacking ambition, direction, and courage, accusing him of being a coward who didn’t aspire for greatness.

The Little Prince once had such a pure dream, unable to comprehend why so many people were so complicated, so impure.

And now, what I’m tirelessly pursuing is nothing more than a pure dream, akin to watering flowers—perhaps one or several dreams.

Related Articles:

  • Exploring the Concept of Yin Yang
  • Zen Buddhism: Understanding the Way

References:

  • Jet Li - Wikipedia
  • Yin Yang - Wikipedia

entry.title: Simple
entry.content:
Many years ago, I might have been someone who delved into various fields of knowledge, and perhaps I still am to this day, but now I’m captivated by a very simple principle: “Keep it simple.”

This principle, originally known as “K.I.S.S” - Keep it simple, stupid.

Albert Einstein once said:

“Everything should be made as simple as possible, but no simpler.”

“It can scarcely be denied that the supreme goal of all theory is to make the irreducible basic elements as simple and as few as possible without having to surrender the adequate representation of a single datum of experience.”

For many things, we often think they are too complex, too complex to understand. In fact, the world can be broken down into simplicity. Zhang Chaoyang once shared this insight.

I recall Fu Peirong discussing Western philosophy in class, mentioning one of Heraclitus’ thoughts: “All things come from fire.”

This perspective may seem erroneous now, but at that time, I found it impressive. He was seeking the most fundamental construction of the world and drew a conclusion.

Perhaps this is one of the origins of materialism, and it’s quite significant.

When discussing logic, we also need to clarify the concept first, as the concept is the most basic unit of semantic understanding.

Back during my student days, when I first encountered chemical molecules and atoms, one day our chemistry teacher told us that both diamond and charcoal are composed of carbon elements. However, diamond is C60, and charcoal is C4, just differing in structural bonds.

In the workplace, we often break down tasks in planning, aiming to simplify tasks to the utmost, to the point where they are crystal clear.

The periodic table of elements is a marvelous thing, and it remains so to this day. Bill Gates has an element wall in his office, reportedly collecting physical samples of all elements except radioactive ones.

I’ve also attempted to understand the simplicity of this world, trying to peel away the layers of its various exteriors.

The world originally might have consisted of individual entities composed of these elements, and then due to various connections, the world gained its rich diversity, forming its shape.

More than 90% of things in this world can be broken down to a simple level of comprehension. If they cannot, it only suggests that we are still unable to understand, unable to delve into the truth and essence, requiring continual exploration.

The world of computers has only 0 and 1. Is it possible that our world is the same?

entry.title: Thinking About the Essence: Universe, Humanity, and the Self - Discovering Life's Meaning and Inner Peace
entry.content:
In my daily reflections, I often ponder profound questions about the universe, essence, and self. I frequently look up at the stars, contemplating the nature of the universe and our place in this vast space. These seemingly distant questions are closely related to our everyday lives.

The Essence and Mysteries of the Universe: Our Place in the Vastness

Every time I gaze at the stars, I am struck by the vastness and mystery of the universe. Although science tells us the universe began with the Big Bang, its origins and future remain full of enigmas. What we know may be just the tip of the iceberg; exploring the universe is an endless journey.

The Nature of Humanity and the Evolution of Civilization: Who Are We?

The development of human civilization evokes complex feelings in me. On one hand, we have created rich cultures and advanced technologies, transforming the world. On the other hand, we face challenges like environmental pollution and social issues. This makes me question what human nature is. Is our civilization truly progressing? These questions drive me to delve into the past, present, and future of humanity.

Exploring Self-Discovery: What Kind of Being Am I?

Now, let’s talk about the self. I often ask myself, who am I? Why do I think and act the way I do? Self-discovery is a complex and evolving process. I realize that my understanding of myself changes over time. Every attempt to understand myself feels like chasing a shadow—confusing yet fascinating.

The Purpose of Life and the Pursuit of Fulfillment: What Constitutes True Inner Satisfaction?

When it comes to the meaning of life, I often reflect. Some people believe that the meaning of life lies in pursuing happiness, but what is happiness? In this challenging world, I have come to understand that the meaning of life may lie in seeking inner satisfaction and peace. This satisfaction does not come from material wealth but from inner harmony and self-realization.

Finding Inner Peace: Achieving Calm in a Busy Life

Finally, I consider inner peace. In today’s fast-paced world, finding inner calm is not easy. However, through simple habits, such as taking time each day to reflect or engage in activities I enjoy, I find brief moments of tranquility. This peace is not an escape from reality but learning to coexist harmoniously with oneself.

Related Articles:

  • Exploring the Essence of the Universe
  • Self-Discovery and the Meaning of Life

References:

  • Universe - Wikipedia
  • Humanity - Wikipedia
  • Self - Wikipedia
  • Purpose of Life - Wikipedia
  • Inner Peace - Wikipedia
